Decision: Use OAuth 2.0 with PKCE flow for mobile authentication
Context/Background: Horizon Financial requires secure authentication for financial data access. Need to balance security with mobile UX.
Options Considered: 1. OAuth 2.0 with PKCE | 2. SAML 2.0 federation | 3. Custom JWT implementation
Rationale: PKCE is the industry standard for mobile apps and avoids exposing client secrets. SAML too heavy for mobile. Custom JWT introduces unnecessary security risk.
Notes: Documented in ADR-007. Horizon Financial security team reviewed and approved.

Decision: Place Customer Portal v2 on hold until DPM Phase 2 completes
Context/Background: Portal v2 depends on 12 API endpoints being rebuilt as part of the data platform migration. Building against unstable APIs would cause significant rework.
Options Considered: 1. Continue portal development against legacy APIs then migrate | 2. Pause portal until new APIs are stable | 3. Build portal with adapter layer for both old and new APIs
Rationale: Option 2 avoids double-handling and rework. Option 3 adds complexity and estimated 6 weeks extra effort. Steering committee agreed delay is preferable to budget overrun.
Impact: High - delays portal launch by approximately 8 weeks
Notes: Product Team initially pushed for Option 3 but agreed after seeing cost-benefit analysis. Portal restart criteria defined: all 12 endpoints passing integration tests.

Decision: Allocate 60% of budget to digital channels and 40% to traditional
Context/Background: Market research shows target demographic (28-45 financial services professionals) increasingly responsive to digital but still engaged with industry publications.
Rationale: Historical campaign data shows 60/40 split delivers optimal cost-per-acquisition for this demographic. Pure digital misses trade publication audience that drives enterprise leads.
Impact: Medium - determines channel strategy and agency brief requirements
Notes: CFO approved allocation. David Kim to build tracking model to measure channel effectiveness.

Decision: Extend rollback window from 48 to 72 hours post-cutover
Context/Background: Original 48-hour rollback window deemed insufficient given the volume of data and number of dependent systems. Compliance team requires additional verification time.
Options Considered: 1. Keep 48-hour window | 2. Extend to 72 hours | 3. Implement parallel-run for 2 weeks
Rationale: 72 hours provides adequate safety net without the significant cost of parallel-run infrastructure. Compliance team confirmed 72 hours meets their audit requirements.
Impact: Medium - requires additional staging environment retention
Notes: Staging environment costs increase by approximately 4K for the extended window. Approved within existing contingency.

Decision: Defer push notification feature from sprint 5 to sprint 7
Context/Background: Sprint 5 capacity reduced due to team member attending conference. Push notifications have external dependency on notification service vendor contract not yet signed.
Options Considered: 1. Include in sprint 5 with overtime | 2. Defer to sprint 6 | 3. Defer to sprint 7
Rationale: Sprint 7 aligns with vendor contract expected signing date (mid-April). Deferring to sprint 6 would still require building against unsigned contract. No overtime for non-critical features.
Impact: Low - feature deferral within acceptable timeline
Notes: Communicated to Horizon Financial. Jonathan Hayes acknowledged deferral with no concerns.
